- A valid collision-free path requires continuous free space from start to goal. Two visible open areas separated by a wall, dense furniture, or a locked gate do not form a usable route even if they are close on the grid.
- When counting obstacles to be overtaken, moved, or jumped over, restrict the count to those objects that actually lie within the chosen lane or path corridor. Objects in adjacent lanes, on sidewalks, or off the route should not be included.
- Maze-like and structured environments (hedge mazes, shelving aisles, fenced corridors) require graph-connectivity reasoning. Coordinate differences alone cannot determine reachability when walls or barriers partition the space.
- For route-comparison questions (shortest vs. longest valid path), each candidate route must be independently verified as collision-free. A longer route is valid only if every segment avoids object collision under the stated movement constraints.
- Crowds, furniture clusters, stacked tires, and parked vehicles often create effective barriers larger than any single object's bounding box. Evaluate the aggregate blocked area, not individual item sizes.

450.spatial_relations_text:
--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Obstacle Avoidance) ---
- An obstacle is any object or structure that physically prevents or impedes collision-free motion along a candidate path. Only objects whose spatial footprint intersects the traversal corridor should be counted as obstacles; co-located but non-blocking scene items are not obstacles.
- Traversability depends on the agent type. Humans can navigate narrower gaps and step over low objects; cars require lane-width clearance and cannot weave through pedestrian gaps; trucks need even wider corridors and higher vertical clearance. Always match the passability test to the stated agent.
- A valid collision-free path requires continuous free space from start to goal. Two visible open areas separated by a wall, dense furniture, or a locked gate do not form a usable route even if they are close on the grid.
- When counting obstacles to be overtaken, moved, or jumped over, restrict the count to those objects that actually lie within the chosen lane or path corridor. Objects in adjacent lanes, on sidewalks, or off the route should not be included.
- Maze-like and structured environments (hedge mazes, shelving aisles, fenced corridors) require graph-connectivity reasoning. Coordinate differences alone cannot determine reachability when walls or barriers partition the space.
- For route-comparison questions (shortest vs. longest valid path), each candidate route must be independently verified as collision-free. A longer route is valid only if every segment avoids object collision under the stated movement constraints.
- Crowds, furniture clusters, stacked tires, and parked vehicles often create effective barriers larger than any single object's bounding box. Evaluate the aggregate blocked area, not individual item sizes.
- Do not assume that absence of a labeled obstacle means the space is free. Scene structure itself—walls, railings, curbs, platform edges—can constrain motion even when not explicitly listed as objects.
